prompt: A vast murmuration of starlings at golden dusk above flat marshland and a wide silver river, sun sitting exactly on the horizon, sky burning amber and deep copper. The murmuration forms one single, cohesive, sweeping ribbon shape across the frame — like a wave or a brushstroke. It moves as one body. Exactly three quarters of the flock are ghost birds: rendered as extremely fine, elegant, silver-white line drawings — like delicate ink etchings or astronomical engravings. Each ghost bird is precisely bird-shaped, wings extended in flight, but consisting only of a thin outline. They are beautiful, not glitchy. Graceful, not broken. They hold formation perfectly within the murmuration shape. In the lower-right portion of the flock — small, dense, precious — roughly one quarter of the birds are solid: dark, warm, physically real starlings with feathers catching the amber light. They are visibly outnumbered. The solid cluster feels like a remnant. The ghost birds and real birds together form one unbroken flock shape. The overwhelming majority of the sky the flock occupies is filled with absence. No text. No labels. No axes. No gradients between states — ghost birds are ghost birds, real birds are real. Photorealistic landscape. Cinematic. Shot on medium format film. Profound, quiet grief.
insight: Global vertebrate wildlife populations have declined by an average of 73% since 1970, leaving only 27% of what once existed.
